title: Benchmarking Retrieval Strategies
concept: Running the same query set through several strategies and comparing recall to pick a winner empirically.

exercises.prompt: From our previous lesson, why is stacking too many retrieval techniques at once risky?

options.text: It increases system latency and creates more potential points of failure.
It guarantees 100% accuracy for every search query.
It automatically lowers database hosting costs.

exercises.explain: The pipeline complexity budget reminds us that adding more steps (like multiple rerankers and translation layers) increases latency and points of failure, which can hurt the overall user experience.

cards.title: Stop Guessing, Start Measuring
cards.body: We know that stacking every retrieval trick makes our system slow and complex. But how do we actually prove which single strategy—or combination—gives us the best search results for our users?

cards.title: What is Retrieval Benchmarking?
cards.body: Benchmarking retrieval strategies means running a fixed, representative set of search queries through different pipeline setups and measuring their success rate (like Recall) using a golden test dataset.

cards.title: The Running Shoe Test
cards.body: Imagine you are running a race to test four different running shoes. You wouldn't just look at them; you would wear each pair, run the exact same 100-meter track, and clock your times to see which one actually makes you fastest.

cards.title: Comparing the Contenders
cards.body: A company tests three setups: Keyword Search, Vector Search, and Hybrid Search. By running 500 test questions, they find that Hybrid Search retrieves 92% of the correct documents (Recall@5), while Vector alone only finds 78%.

cards.title: The "Complex is Better" Trap
cards.body: Many developers assume the most advanced, multi-stage pipeline is always the best choice. In reality, simpler setups often win on speed and accuracy once you run them against a real-world test set.

cards.title: The Benchmark Arena
cards.body: See how benchmarking pits different strategies against a single, standardized test track to reveal the true winner.

left.text: 100 fixed queries ran against Strategy A, B, and C.

right.text: Strategy B wins with 95% Recall and 40ms latency.

exercises.prompt: When benchmarking three different retrieval strategies, what is a golden rule of the experiment?

options.text: Run different queries on each strategy so they don't get bored.
Use the exact same set of queries and documents for every strategy.
Ask ChatGPT to guess which strategy will perform better.
Only test the strategy that is the cheapest to run.

exercises.explain: To make a benchmark fair and scientific, we must run the exact same set of test queries across all the different retrieval configurations we want to compare.

exercises.prompt: True or False: When benchmarking retrieval strategies, we should measure both search quality (Recall) and execution speed (Latency).
exercises.explain: A pipeline with high accuracy is useless if it takes 10 seconds to return results. Benchmarking should measure both search quality (like Recall) and system speed (Latency).

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the definition of this crucial search metric.
exercises.sentence: We measure Recall to see what percentage of the target documents our pipeline successfully retrieved.
exercises.explain: Recall measures the percentage of correct, relevant documents that our retrieval strategy actually managed to find and pull from the database.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following describes an AI-assisted evaluation system for benchmarking retrieval?

options.text: A developer writes a Python script to print out retrieval results to the terminal.
A product manager reads through 10 retrieved documents and writes down their feedback in a spreadsheet.
An automated evaluation script sends queries to three pipelines, and uses a second LLM to grade whether the retrieved chunks match the reference answers.

exercises.explain: Option C describes an automated evaluation setup where an LLM judges the relevance of retrieved documents generated by different pipeline configurations. Options A and B are manual developer tasks.

exercises.prompt: You want to decide whether to use a hybrid search pipeline or a simple keyword pipeline for your customer support bot. What is the most scientific way to set up your benchmark test?

options.text: Run a fixed set of 100 benchmark queries against each pipeline, using a test set of known correct documents to calculate Recall.
Run different queries for each pipeline and ask a colleague which results look more interesting.
Evaluate the pipelines using entirely different document collections to see which collection is easier to search.
Always choose the most complex pipeline because more steps automatically guarantee better benchmarking scores.

exercises.explain: Benchmarking requires holding the query set and the evaluation data constant while swapping only the retrieval pipeline components. Changing the documents or queries midway ruins the fairness of the experiment.
